text.title: Banking Core — Data Residency Rules Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every application of banking-core-data-residency-rules enforces. Each rule has a statement, a rationale anchored to industry practice, and a source citation.

rule.statement: Every PII data class is tagged with its jurisdiction-of-origin AND the set of permitted processing locations; processing outside that set is forbidden by default.
rule.rationale: Without tagging, residency becomes post-hoc lawyering; tagging makes it a CI-checkable invariant.

rule.statement: Every cross-border transfer ships with: (a) legal basis (adequacy / SCC / BCR), (b) Transfer Impact Assessment, (c) supplementary measures if Schrems II triggers.
rule.rationale: Post-Schrems II, EU→US transfers without TIA are presumptively unlawful.

rule.statement: Processor changes (new vendor / new region) require a documented residency-impact review BEFORE production rollout.
rule.rationale: Vendor changes are the dominant source of unintended residency drift.

rule.statement: Supervisor notification triggers are pre-mapped: incident type → supervisor → SLA → template.
rule.rationale: At incident time there is no bandwidth to figure out which regulator to call; the map removes that step.

rule.statement: Backups and replicas live in the same residency envelope as primary data — unless explicitly exempted with a documented basis.
rule.rationale: Backups are the most-frequent residency-violation vector (silent replication to default-US region).

rule.statement: If preconditions in `Applies If` are not all true, skip this methodology and route to the adjacent one referenced in Related.
rule.rationale: A skip rule keeps the decision tree closed: every branch ends at a real rule id.
